humanities_role_explanation: The evaluation uses 18th-century Russian Civil font books as test content, and the benchmark is specifically designed to assess diplomatic transcription quality for historical corpus creation and Digital Humanities workflows.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Multimodal OCR evaluation combining scanned images of historical pages or lines with textual prompts to generate transcriptions in natural language.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluating character-level transcription accuracy of historical scripts, specifically testing the preservation of period-accurate orthographic symbols and identifying systematic temporal errors.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Specifically designed to evaluate OCR performance on historical Russian Civil font documents rather than general-purpose LLM capabilities.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluated 12 models including Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, GPT-4o, GPT-4.1, o4-mini, Gemini 2.0/2.5 series, Qwen2.5-VL-72B, and Llama-4 models.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The primary systems being benchmarked are generative multimodal LLMs tested on direct OCR transcription and post-OCR correction tasks.

arts_humanities_data.note: Focuses on historical printed books (fiction, religion, history, science) in 18th-century Russian Civil font, mapping to Language and Linguistics, History, and Literature.

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses standard OCR metrics (CER, WER, CaseER) alongside newly proposed metrics: Historical Character Preservation Rate (HCPR) and Archaic Insertion Rate (AIR).

english_language.note: The scientific paper is written in English, though the benchmark corpus is in Russian.

summary: This paper presents an evaluation methodology and a novel 1,029-page dataset for assessing LLMs on historical document OCR, specifically focusing on 18th-century Russian Civil font. It introduces novel metrics (HCPR and AIR) to capture period-specific errors and temporal biases. The study reveals that while top-performing LLMs outperform traditional OCR baselines, they suffer from 'over-historicization' by inserting obsolete, pre-reform characters.
key_findings: Gemini-2.5-Pro achieved the best transcription accuracy (CER of 3.36% and WER of 4.69% in full-page mode) and the highest consistency. Many models displayed 'over-historicization' (e.g., GPT-4o inserting archaic pre-Petrine characters in 59% of documents). Post-OCR correction using LLMs was found to degrade rather than improve transcription accuracy.
